subject: Geology
title: Aftershocks and Foreshocks
description: A quick, practical guide to Aftershocks and Foreshocks.

hook.text: The main quake ends, but the danger isn't over. Aftershocks can continue for weeks, toppling weakened buildings.

buildup.text: Aftershocks are smaller earthquakes following the main shock, caused by the crust adjusting to the new stress.

discovery.text: Foreshocks precede the main shock, but we only identify them as foreshocks after the bigger event happens.

twist.text: There's no reliable way to tell if a quake is a foreshock until afterwards. Every earthquake could be a warning.

climax.text: Aftershocks follow predictable decay patterns. Foreshocks remain maddeningly unpredictable and unreliable as warnings.

punchline.text: The shaking doesn't end with the main event.

quiz.question: Why are foreshocks hard to use as warnings?
options: We can only identify them after the main shock occurs
